pattern: Single-character conversions are just weighted edges on a 26-letter graph, so the cheapest way to turn one letter into another anywhere in the string is an all-pairs shortest path computed once and reused for every position.
hints: Treat the 26 lowercase letters as nodes and every (original[i], changed[i], cost[i]) triple as a directed edge; if a pair repeats, only the cheapest edge between those two letters ever matters.
Since the letters form a tiny fixed set, precompute the shortest path between every pair of letters once (Floyd-Warshall on a 26x26 table works nicely here) instead of searching per character.

Walk source and target together position by position: matching characters cost nothing, and every mismatch costs exactly the precomputed shortest distance between those two letters -- if any position's distance is infinite, the whole conversion is impossible.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, several single-character conversions chained together
statement example, cheaper to route through an intermediate letter
source already equals target, zero cost
single character strings with a direct edge
two competing edges between the same pair, only the cheaper one should be used
a conversion that requires going through two intermediate letters
a cycle exists in the graph but the needed direction is unreachable

stress.description: long strings with a random dense set of single-character conversion edges
